sources.notes: Official editorial confirms decomposing the graph by bridges and analyzing parity constraints inside 2-edge-connected components.
Chinese solution used to cross-check the bridge and odd-cycle cases.

annotation.summary: Decide/count assignments under XOR-like graph constraints.
annotation.constraints: The graph is large, so the solution needs linear graph decomposition and bitwise independent reasoning.
annotation.core_idea: Bridge edges separate independent constraints. Inside each 2-edge-connected component, parity along cycles determines whether the component behaves bipartitely or has an odd-cycle freedom that fixes more values. DFS/Tarjan decomposition plus bipartite checks turn the original assignment condition into per-component choices.
annotation.complexity: O((n+m) log A) or O(n+m) per bit depending on implementation
tricks: Use bipartite versus odd-cycle structure to decide degrees of freedom.

solution_variants.name: bridge-components-and-parity
solution_variants.summary: Run bridge decomposition, inspect bipartiteness/odd cycles in each component, and combine independent bit choices.

tags.evidence: Bridge decomposition is the main graph reduction that separates independent assignment constraints.
The behavior of each non-bridge component depends on whether its parity graph is bipartite or contains an odd cycle.
The assignment constraints can be evaluated independently over bits.
DFS/Tarjan traversal is used to find bridges and color components.
After graph reduction, the answer is a product/sum of component choice counts.
